title: Generic on-premises air-gapped deployment platform
description: Baseline infrastructure pattern for deploying applications in air-gapped, on-premises environments with no external network connectivity. Establishes network isolation, hardware-bound resource limits, local PKI, and mandatory audit trails for regulated and classified workloads.

cost.costNotes: Hardware procurement, colocation, and maintenance costs dominate; no cloud provider billing.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($500): Small workload on existing shared hardware with minimal dedicated infrastructure. Max ($30,000): Dedicated HA bare-metal cluster with redundant networking, enterprise storage (SAN/NAS with replication), UPS, and colocation fees for a mid-size production deployment.
provenance.adoptionCost: High complexity: network isolation design, hardware provisioning, OS hardening, air-gapped patching workflow design and documentation, PKI bootstrap, and training. Typically 150-250 hours of senior engineering work at $75-100/hour blended rate, yielding $11,000-18,000 with AI assistance.
provenance.licenseCost: Open-source OS and tooling (Linux, Kubernetes, containerd). No cloud-provider licensing. Commercial support contracts (RHEL, SUSE) are optional and priced separately.

provides.reasoning: Pattern establishes a fully isolated on-prem environment with no external network connectivity, directly enabling air-gapped deployment for any workload.
Core property of the pattern: workloads run in a physically isolated network segment with no internet egress.
Pattern encapsulates all baseline concerns for hosting applications on customer-owned or collocated hardware.
On-premises airgapped deployment is the pattern that establishes and owns the physical infrastructure layer.

requires.reasoning: Air-gapped deployment requires customer-owned or collocated hardware rather than cloud VMs.
Without cloud snapshots or managed backups, applications must rely on locally operated backup-restore procedures for disaster recovery.
Regulated air-gapped environments mandate local audit log collection and immutable retention without external SIEM dependency.

supports_constraints.reason: This pattern is exclusively for on-premises environments; cloud or hybrid deployments are out of scope.
Infrastructure pattern independent of application programming language.
Air-gapped hosting applies to all platform types; application type does not change the deployment environment.

supports_nfr.reason: On-prem deployment is compatible with both PII and non-PII workloads.
Air-gapped environment with local data residency can meet GDPR requirements when properly configured.
On-prem air-gapped environments are commonly used for HIPAA workloads due to data residency and access control requirements.
Local data storage and access controls support CCPA compliance when the application layer enforces data subject rights.
Air-gapped platform with audit logging and change control workflows can satisfy SOX requirements.
On-prem platform is compatible with either read-your-writes or eventual consistency models; determined by the storage layer.
Local storage with RAID and cross-site replication can satisfy strict durability; compatibility depends on storage hardware configuration.

requires_nfr.reason: Generic on-prem hardware without a managed cloud SLA is bounded to three-nines (99.9%) availability. Planned maintenance windows, hardware failure response, and manual patching in air-gapped environments prevent higher guarantees without significant dedicated hot-standby investment.
On-prem internal datacenter network round-trip introduces at least 2ms p95 latency even for collocated services on the same rack.
Network jitter, shared storage I/O contention, and OS interrupt latency on physical hardware result in at least 10ms p99 latency.

Physical hardware failure recovery (IPMI access, re-seat, failover to standby node) and manual intervention in air-gapped environments means RTO cannot be less than 15 minutes without dedicated hot-standby infrastructure and automated failover.
Local backup and replication schedules in air-gapped environments impose at least 5 minutes RPO; sub-minute RPO requires dedicated synchronous replication hardware.

Hardware-bound CPU, memory, and I/O limits on typical on-prem server clusters cap throughput at roughly 100,000 jobs/second (360M/hour). Exceeding this requires significant horizontal scaling investment.

Air-gapped environments mandate local audit logging; without cloud-native SIEM, all security events must be captured locally for forensic capability and regulatory compliance. Applications on this platform must emit auditable events.

deployment_model.description: How applications are deployed on physical hardware. bare-metal: Direct OS execution, highest performance and isolation. vm: Hypervisor-based virtualization, flexible resource allocation. containerized: Container orchestration (Kubernetes/k3s), best density and portability in air-gapped environments.

ha_mode.description: High-availability topology. single-site: All hardware in one location (simpler but single point of failure for site disasters). multi-site-active-passive: Primary site plus warm standby (RTO ~15-30min for site failure). multi-site-active-active: Both sites active with synchronous replication (highest availability, highest cost and complexity).

network_isolation_level.description: Degree of network isolation. full-airgap: No internet connectivity; all updates via physical media (USB/DVD). partial-airgap: Controlled one-way data diode for specific approved feeds (e.g., threat intelligence). dmz: Demilitarized zone with firewall-controlled internet access limited to approved update servers.

backup_strategy.description: Data protection approach. local-snapshots: On-site storage snapshots (fast restore, vulnerable to site disaster). tape-offsite: Tape backups transported off-site (secure, slow restore, meets air-gap requirements). cross-site-replication: Real-time replication to second site (requires multi-site HA configuration).

patch_delivery.description: How OS and software updates enter the air-gapped environment. manual-usb: Updates shipped on USB/DVD after offline scanning (highest isolation, most labor-intensive). air-gap-jump-server: Internet-connected staging server scans and transfers approved packages via controlled process (semi-automated). one-way-data-diode: Hardware data diode allows inbound-only transfer from approved external feed (automated, cryptographically verified).
